---
title: "Lectures"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    page-layout: full
---

Lecture slide decks are available in html format.

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {.grid .gap-3}
:::::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-sm-4}
::::: {.card .h-100}
::: card-header
Lecture 1
:::

::: card-body
##### Statistics and Samples {.card-title}

Introduction to populations, samples, and the role of sampling in statistical inference.

[Go to Lecture](slides/lecture-01-statistics-samples/){.btn .btn-primary}
:::
:::::
::::::

:::::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-sm-4}
::::: {.card .h-100}
::: card-header
Lecture 2
:::

::: card-body
##### Data and Variables {.card-title}

Types of variables, their roles in analysis, and why correlation does not imply causation.

[Go to Lecture](slides/lecture-02-data-and-variables/){.btn .btn-primary}
:::
:::::
::::::

:::::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-sm-4}
::::: {.card .h-100}
::: card-header
Lecture 3
:::

::: card-body
##### Effective data visualization {.card-title}

Key principles of good graphics, focusing on clarity, honesty, and making patterns easy to see.

[Go to Lecture](slides/lecture-03-effective-data-visualization/){.btn .btn-primary}
:::
:::::
::::::

:::::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-sm-4}
::::: {.card .h-100}
::: card-header
Lecture 4
:::

::: card-body
##### Visualizing Data Types {.card-title}

Common visualization types and their suitability for different data types and analytical goals.

[Go to Lecture](slides/lecture-04-visualizing-data-types/){.btn .btn-primary}
:::
:::::
::::::

:::::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-sm-4}
::::: {.card .h-100}
::: card-header
Lecture 5
:::

::: card-body
##### Describing Data {.card-title}

Describing data using measures of location, variability, and appropriate numerical precision.

[Go to Lecture](slides/lecture-05-describing-data/){.btn .btn-primary}
:::
:::::
::::::

:::::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-sm-4}
::::: {.card .h-100}
::: card-header
Lecture 6
:::

::: card-body
##### Estimating with uncertainty {.card-title}

Estimating population parameters and their uncertainty from sample data

[Go to Lecture](slides/lecture-06-uncertainty/){.btn .btn-primary}
:::
:::::
::::::

:::::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-sm-4}
::::: {.card .h-100}
::: card-header
Lecture 7
:::

::: card-body
##### Probability {.card-title}

Introduces probability theory as the foundation of statistical reasoning, focusing on conditional probability, independence, and Bayes’ theorem.

[Go to Lecture](slides/lecture-07-probability/){.btn .btn-primary}
:::
:::::
::::::

:::::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-sm-4}
::::: {.card .h-100}
::: card-header
Lecture 8
:::

::: card-body
##### Hypothesis testing {.card-title}

An introduction to hypothesis testing in biology using a proportion example to illustrate null models, $P$-values, error types, and scientific interpretation.

[Go to Lecture](slides/lecture-08-hypothesis-testing/){.btn .btn-primary}
:::
:::::
::::::

:::::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-sm-4}
::::: {.card .h-100}
::: card-header
Lecture 9
:::

::: card-body
##### Analyzing proportions {.card-title}

Introduces the binomial distribution for modeling binary outcomes and develops the statistical tools needed to estimate and draw inference about population proportions.

[Go to Lecture](slides/lecture-09-analyzing-proportions/){.btn .btn-primary}
:::
:::::
::::::

:::::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-sm-4}
::::: {.card .h-100}
::: card-header
Lecture 10
:::

::: card-body
##### Fitting Probability Models to Frequency Data {.card-title}

Using proportional probability models and the chi-squared goodness-of-fit test to evaluate whether observed categorical frequency data fit expectations under a null hypothesis.

[Go to Lecture](slides/lecture-10-fitting-probability-models-to-frequency-data/){.btn .btn-primary}
:::
:::::
::::::

:::::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-sm-4}
::::: {.card .h-100}
::: card-header
Lecture 11
:::

::: card-body
##### Using the Poisson Distribution to Test Randomness in Count Data {.card-title}

Introduces the Poisson distribution as a model for random counts in time or space and shows how to test whether observed data fit this model using a chi-square goodness-of-fit test.

[Go to Lecture](slides/lecture-11-poisson-distribution/){.btn .btn-primary}
:::
:::::
::::::

:::::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-sm-4}
::::: {.card .h-100}
::: card-header
Lecture 12
:::

::: card-body
##### Contingency Analysis {.card-title}

Introduces methods for exploring the association between two categorical variables using contingency tables. We develop conditional probability (risk), relative risk, odds, the odds ratio, and the chi-squared test for independence.

[Go to Lecture](slides/lecture-12-contingency-analysis/){.btn .btn-primary}
:::
:::::
::::::

:::::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-sm-4}
::::: {.card .h-100}
::: card-header
Lecture 13
:::

::: card-body
##### The Normal Distribution {.card-title}

Introduces the normal distribution, its properties, and how Z-scores are used to calculate probabilities. Also explains the sampling distribution of the mean and how sample size affects its spread.

[Go to Lecture](slides/lecture-13-normal-distribution/){.btn .btn-primary}
:::
:::::
::::::

:::::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-sm-4}
::::: {.card .h-100}
::: card-header
Lecture 14
:::

::: card-body
##### Inference for Population Means {.card-title}

[Confidence Intervals and t-Tests]{.card-subtitle}

Introduces statistical inference for normally distributed data, including confidence intervals for the mean and hypothesis tests (one-sample, independent two-sample, and paired t-tests) used to evaluate differences in population means.

[Go to Lecture](slides/lecture-14-t-tests-and-ci/){.btn .btn-primary}
:::
:::::
::::::

:::::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-sm-4}
::::: {.card .h-100}
::: card-header
Lecture 15
:::

::: card-body
##### Comparing Two Means {.card-title}

Introduces statistical methods for comparing two means, including Welch two-sample and paired t-tests, confidence intervals for mean differences, and appropriate study designs for two-sample inference.

[Go to Lecture](slides/lecture-15-comparing-two-means/){.btn .btn-primary}
:::
:::::
::::::

:::::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-sm-4}
::::: {.card .h-100}
::: card-header
Lecture 16
:::

::: card-body
##### Handling Violations of Assumptions {.card-title}

Handling violations of statistical assumptions, including when to proceed, transform data, or use alternative methods for non-normal data.

[Go to Lecture](slides/lecture-16-handling-violations-of-assumptions/){.btn .btn-primary}
:::
:::::
::::::

:::::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-sm-4}
::::: {.card .h-100}
::: card-header
Lecture 17
:::

::: card-body
##### Choosing a test {.card-title}

Summarizes the decision process for choosing an appropriate statistical test based on the research question, data types, and study design. Provides a flowchart to guide test selection.

[Go to Lecture](slides/lecture-17-choosing-a-test){.btn .btn-primary}
:::
:::::
::::::

:::::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-sm-4}
::::: {.card .h-100}
::: card-header
Lecture 18
:::

::: card-body
##### Designing Experiments {.card-title}

Introduces how well-designed experiments enable causal inference by reducing bias and sampling error, while contrasting their strengths with observational studies and outlining practical strategies for study design and sample size planning

[Go to Lecture](slides/lecture-18-designing-experiments/){.btn .btn-primary}
:::
:::::
::::::

:::::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-sm-4}
::::: {.card .h-100}
::: card-header
Lecture 19
:::

::: card-body
##### Comparing means of more than two groups {.card-title}

Introduces Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) as a method for comparing means across multiple groups by partitioning variation and testing whether between-group differences exceed within-group variation.

[Go to Lecture](slides/lecture-19-comparing-more-than-two-means/){.btn .btn-primary}
:::
:::::
::::::

:::::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-sm-4}
::::: {.card .h-100}
::: card-header
Lecture 20
:::

::: card-body
##### Correlation between numerical variables {.card-title}

Introduces correlation as a measure of the strength and direction of association between two numerical variables, including interpretation of Pearson’s r, confidence intervals and hypothesis tests, assumptions, common pitfalls, and correlation matrices.

[Go to Lecture](slides/lecture-20-correlation/){.btn .btn-primary}
:::
:::::
::::::

:::::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-sm-4}
::::: {.card .h-100}
::: card-header
Lecture 21
:::

::: card-body
##### Regression {.card-title}

Introduces simple linear regression for modeling relationships between two numerical variables, making and evaluating predictions, interpreting model results, checking assumptions, and recognizing common pitfalls such as extrapolation.

[Go to Lecture](slides/lecture-21-regression/){.btn .btn-primary}
:::
:::::
::::::

:::::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-sm-4}
::::: {.card .h-100}
::: card-header
Lecture 22
:::

::: card-body
##### Analyzing Multiple Factors {.card-title}

Introduces linear models for analyzing response variables influenced by multiple explanatory variables, including categorical and numerical predictors, interactions, blocking, factorial designs, and ANCOVA.

[Go to Lecture](slides/lecture-22-multiple-factors/){.btn .btn-primary}
:::
:::::
::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
